title: Child Wellbeing and Education Research
text:
Welcome
This guide is the primary reference for Child Wellbeing and Education Research (CWER) team members. It covers the team’s mission and philosophy, member roles and responsibilities, tools and setup, data management standards, and statistical workflows.
Whether you are a new research assistant getting oriented or a returning team member looking up a convention, this is the place to start.

title: Child Wellbeing and Education Research
text:
How to Use This Guide
New team members should work through Part I (About CWER) and Part II (Tools & Setup) in order during their first week. These chapters cover what the team does, how it operates, and how to get your accounts and software configured.
Returning members can use the sidebar navigation to jump directly to the section they need. The workflows chapters are intended as ongoing reference material.
This guide is a living document. It is reviewed and updated at least once per year each June. If you notice something that is out of date or missing, contact Mike or Joanna.

title: 1  Mission & Philosophy
text:
1.1 Mission
We conduct applied research on factors that are linked to outcomes concerned with the academic, social, behavioral, and psychological wellbeing of children and youth. We primarily draw on research theory and methods from the fields of psychology, education, and interdisciplinary areas such as human development, evaluation, implementation science, and translational science.
What we mean by applied is that our work focuses on addressing practical problems and producing evidence that addresses practical questions. Further, we aim to make research useable and actionable. Applied research differs from basic research, which focuses more on advancing scientific knowledge in areas that researchers are curious about. While in theory these types of research are distinct, in practice they sometimes overlap, which is the case with our work. Although we have a strong lean towards the applied end of research, we believe that basic research is essential for addressing gaps in our scientific knowledge, and we aim to produce work that bridges the goals of applied and basic research.
Areas of research and evaluation we engage in include:

title: 1  Mission & Philosophy
text:
Early childhood education
Social and emotional competencies
Psychosocial contributors to achievement gaps
Academic engagement (behavioral and psychological)
Parental engagement
School and home interventions and programs

title: 1  Mission & Philosophy
text:
1.2 Philosophy

The first principle is that you must not fool yourself — and you are the easiest person to fool.
— Richard Feynman

The guiding principles of our team are to conduct research that is truthful, rigorous, and collaborative.

1.2.1 Truthfulness
Doing truthful research means engaging in an honest search for answers to questions and allowing for our conclusions to be driven by evidence rather than our individual opinions and desires. This does not mean one needs to approach the work free of values. To the contrary, we care a great deal about solving social problems, but to do so, we believe that an accurate understanding of the nature of such problems and effective means of addressing them is key.
Sometimes we will encounter research findings that run counter to what we initially thought. We must be open to revising our thinking in light of such findings rather than seeking ways to fit the findings into our preexisting conceptual models. In other words, we should never set out to “prove” anything. We should aim to produce the best evidence possible for answering our research questions and see where the evidence takes us.


1.2.2 Rigor
What we mean by rigorous is using the best practices in research methodology and statistics to address research questions given the feasibility constraints of the project. These approaches can range from descriptive research to randomized controlled trials. Doing this requires staying on top of rapidly developing methodological and statistical techniques and sometimes consulting with others who have more expertise in these areas.
We place a strong emphasis on measurement for a couple of reasons. First, the nature of our work often involves complex concepts that are challenging to capture, such as social-emotional competencies, academic engagement, and achievement. To capture these concepts well, we need sound theory and statistics (psychometrics). Second, the quality of research results is largely dependent upon the quality of measurement. If measurement is poor, everything to follow will also be poor. As the saying goes, garbage in, garbage out.


1.2.3 Collaboration
Due to the applied nature of our work, collaboration is regularly an important aspect of producing research that is accurate and useful. Partners often bring critical insights and expertise regarding the contextual factors associated with research questions, as well as valuable information about constraints of the research setting that may limit the type of research or interventions that can be implemented. In addition to helping make research more useable, collaboration adds to our scientific knowledge by advancing our understanding of the contextual factors associated with the outcomes our team focuses on.
The degree to which we collaborate ranges from minimal engagement — such as in certain evaluation contexts where we operate at arm’s length — to high engagement where collaboration is central to the nature of the work, such as our early childhood research-practice partnership, the Partnership for Early Education Research (PEER), and the Bridgeport Social and Emotional Learning Initiative. In most cases, it is important for us to maintain scientific independence while remaining genuinely responsive to the practical needs and insights of our partners. This balance is what makes our work both rigorous and useful.

title: 2  Roles & Responsibilities
text:
2.1 Director
Michael J. Strambler, Ph.D.
One of the main responsibilities of the director is to ensure the team has the appropriate funding, personnel, procedures, and physical and social resources to function well. This requires playing an active role in establishing and supervising research projects and acquiring the funding to support them. The director is also responsible for supervising and supporting the professional development of the assistant/associate director, postdoctoral associates/fellows, and research associates. Finally, the director is responsible for ensuring that the team maintains high ethical standards, including the ethical treatment of participants, the protection of sensitive data, and a respectful, open, and professional team culture. When problems arise in team culture, ethics, or research design, the director is responsible for addressing them and getting the team back on track.

title: 2  Roles & Responsibilities
text:
2.2 Assistant Director
A distinguishing characteristic of the Assistant Director is that they are a key thought partner on the team’s mission and focus. The Assistant Director is expected to make substantive contributions to planning around the types of projects we take on, ensuring that they are consistent with our mission and philosophy. Another key responsibility involves co-directing and managing projects with the Director. This includes developing work plans for projects, pressing the team regarding progress and deadlines, establishing and maintaining IRB protocols, and planning for grant proposals. Supervisory responsibilities include hiring and supervising research assistants for the team.

title: 2  Roles & Responsibilities
text:
2.3 Research Associate
Research Associates have advanced research, data analytic, evaluation, and/or project management skills. The Research Associate applies these skills to specific projects in collaboration with the Director or Assistant Director. The responsibilities of the Research Associate are project-specific, but may involve data analysis, measurement development, report writing, project management, and research design. Research Associates are expected to stay current on new developments in their skill set.

title: 2  Roles & Responsibilities
text:
2.4 Postdoctoral Fellows and Associates
Although postdocs are trainees, they are also considered key colleagues of the CWER team. Postdocs have two main responsibilities: (1) to clarify and move towards their training goals with the support of the director, and (2) to contribute to assigned research projects. For the first responsibility, postdocs should take active steps to seek advice and professional experiences from the director and other appropriate mentors. They should also participate in relevant training seminars in grant writing, professional development, and research methods. Regarding project contributions, postdocs are expected to bring new skills to the team that will help improve the work. The director will assist postdocs in finding a balance between working on CWER projects and establishing their professional independence.

title: 2  Roles & Responsibilities
text:
2.5 Research Assistants
Research assistants (RAs) can be paid or volunteer team members who are brought on to support the research of specific projects. RA responsibilities are project-specific and can include data management and processing, data collection, data analysis (usually less advanced techniques), literature searches and reviews, documentation, meeting facilitation, focus groups, interviews, and report writing.

title: 3  Being a Team Member
text:
3.1 Human Research Training
Every member must complete and stay current with the appropriate human research trainings, including HIPAA, CITI basic training, and any other related Yale trainings. You can find this information here.

title: 3  Being a Team Member
text:
3.2 Data Security and Privacy
Collecting data from someone is like collecting a part of their person, and we have an ethical obligation to treat this information with the utmost care. Accordingly, we take data security very seriously. Data of moderate or high risk should be handled with the procedures described below (see Yale’s definitions of risk here).

If you are not sure whether data are moderate or high-risk, ask either Mike or Joanna before you do anything — or just treat the data as high risk. It is always better to be more cautious when in doubt. Check the IRB protocol and data sharing agreements on issues regarding data protection and sharing.
Moderate and high-risk data can only be saved in the following places: (1) the Yale server (Storage @ Yale); (2) Yale Secure Box (this is different from “regular” Yale Box); (3) Yale-managed Microsoft OneDrive or SharePoint; (4) temporarily on a Yale-managed encrypted hard drive; or (5) temporarily on a Yale-issued IronKey flash drive.
Saving data on Yale encrypted hard drives or IronKey flash drives should only be done when you do not have access to the internet, or you need to transfer data from one location to another. In such cases, immediately transfer data to the server or Secure Box when you have internet access.
Never electronically transmit data with personally identifiable information through any means other than Yale’s Secure File Transfer (or another secure FTP process when partners are transmitting files). Although Yale has a Microsoft Outlook email encryption feature, never use this for transmitting moderate or high-risk data — it is too easy to make a mistake.
Yale issues free high-security flash drives upon request. It is a good idea to have one on hand in case you need to temporarily store sensitive data. This is secure enough to store high-risk data, but only for transporting data from one secure system to another.

title: 3  Being a Team Member
text:
3.3 Open Science & Research Integrity
In line with our philosophy of truthfulness, we aim to practice open science. The term open science encompasses many concepts, including sharing data and materials, using best practices in research design and statistics, and promoting research reproducibility and replicability. The way we like to think of these practices is engaging in research integrity — research that is transparent and credible.
Although we do not always have the option to share data given partners’ privacy concerns, we make every effort to produce research that is reproducible, meaning that someone else with the same access to our data could produce identical findings using the study information we provide. This is also useful for other researchers to examine replicability, or the production of similar findings using a separate dataset.
Some of the practices we aim to use include:

Sharing as many study materials as possible (see OSF in the Tools chapter)
Clearly documenting and sharing analysis code when appropriate
Using methods appropriate for communicating meaningful, interpretable, and accurate findings
Establishing data analysis plans prior to data collection to:

Ensure we collect the proper data and have an adequate sample size to address the research questions
Help avoid biased practices like HARKing (hypothesizing after results are known) and p-hacking (intentionally or unintentionally tweaking analyses to reach statistical significance)

title: 4  Tools Overview
text:
4.1 MS Teams
All CWER members should set up accounts with OSF, Google Drive, and GitHub, and should be added to the group’s MS Teams workspace. The sections below describe each tool and how we use it.
MS Teams is our primary channel for day-to-day communication about projects. It is where you will find project-specific channels, announcements, and quick questions that do not warrant an email. New team members will be added to the CWER Teams workspace by Mike or Joanna during onboarding — you do not need to set this up yourself. Access Teams through your Yale account at teams.microsoft.com or via the desktop app.

title: 4  Tools Overview
text:
4.2 Storage @ Yale
Storage @ Yale is what we use as a shared server for storing project files. You will need to be granted access to the folders for the specific projects you are working on. Contact Mike or Joanna to request access.

title: 4  Tools Overview
text:
4.3 Google Shared Drive
We use Google Shared Drive primarily for taking notes during meetings.
Setup: Users who do not already have an Eli Apps account should contact ITS to request a “no-mail Eli Apps account” associated with their NetID. Users who have an Eli Apps account should send a request to research.computing@yale.edu to ask that they enable Team Drive for their Eli Apps account. See https://docs.ycrc.yale.edu/data/google-drive/ for more information.

title: 4  Tools Overview
text:
4.4 Open Science Framework (OSF)
We use OSF to publicly share information about our work and occasionally for project management. OSF is where we post study materials, preregistrations, and other publicly shareable content in line with our open science commitments. If you are new to OSF, the OSF 101 video is a good starting point.

title: 4  Tools Overview
text:
4.5 GitHub
We use GitHub for version control and collaboration on analysis code. All analysis projects should have a GitHub repository stored under the cwer-yale organization. GitHub allows us to track changes to code over time, collaborate without overwriting each other’s work, and maintain a complete history of analytical decisions.
New team members should request access to the cwer-yale GitHub organization from Mike or Joanna. If you are new to Git and GitHub, see the Git & GitHub chapter of this guide for a walkthrough (coming soon).

title: 5  Git & GitHub
text:
5.1 What Are Git and GitHub?
Git is version control software that tracks changes to files over time. Every time you save a snapshot of your work (called a commit), Git records what changed, when, and who made the change. This means you can always go back to an earlier version of your code, see exactly what changed between two versions, and understand the full history of an analysis.
GitHub is a website that hosts Git repositories in the cloud. Where Git runs on your computer, GitHub is the remote backup and collaboration hub. When you push your work to GitHub, it is saved online and accessible to your teammates.
Think of it this way: Git is the system that tracks your changes locally, and GitHub is where those changes get backed up and shared.

title: 5  Git & GitHub
text:
5.2 Why We Use It
For CWER projects, Git and GitHub serve three purposes:

Backup. Code and analysis scripts are backed up to GitHub automatically every time you push. You will never lose work to a crashed hard drive or an accidentally deleted file.
History. Every commit is a record of what changed and why. When you return to a project after six months, or when a new team member inherits your work, the commit history explains the evolution of the analysis.
Collaboration. Multiple people can work on the same project without overwriting each other’s work. GitHub keeps everything organized under the cwer-yale organization so that lab projects belong to the lab, not to any individual’s personal account.

title: 5  Git & GitHub
text:
5.3 The cwer-yale Organization
All CWER analysis projects live under the cwer-yale GitHub organization, not under personal accounts. This ensures continuity — if a team member leaves, the project stays accessible to the lab. When you start a new analysis project, create the repository in cwer-yale, not in your personal GitHub account.
To get access to cwer-yale, contact Mike or Joanna with your GitHub username and they will add you.

title: 5  Git & GitHub
text:
5.4 Getting Set Up
There are three ways to interact with Git and GitHub: through your IDE, through GitHub Desktop, or through the terminal.
An IDE (Integrated Development Environment) is the application you use to write and run code — for CWER that means Positron or Cursor. Both have built-in Git integration that lets you stage, commit, push, and pull directly from within the editor without switching to another application. This is the most convenient approach for day-to-day work and the one we recommend for most team members, since you are already in the IDE when you are doing analysis.
GitHub Desktop is a standalone visual application for managing Git operations through a point-and-click interface. It is a good option if you want a dedicated app for Git work, or if you are doing more complex operations like branch management.
The terminal is the most flexible approach and is necessary for initial setup steps like configuring Git and authenticating with GitHub. Throughout this guide, setup commands are given as terminal commands. For day-to-day operations like committing and pushing, we show both the terminal and IDE approaches — use whichever feels most natural.
You can switch freely between all three approaches — they all interact with the same underlying Git repository.

title: 6  IDEs
text:
6.1 Positron
An IDE (Integrated Development Environment) is the application you use to write and run code. While you could write R or Stata code in a basic text editor, an IDE adds features that make the work significantly easier: syntax highlighting, error detection, an integrated console for running code interactively, file management, Git integration, and AI assistance. For most researchers, the IDE is where they spend the majority of their working time.
We recommend two IDEs: Positron for most team members, and Cursor for those who want deeper AI integration in their coding workflow.
Positron is developed by Posit, the same company that developed RStudio. It is designed specifically for data science work and has great support for R, Quarto documents, and the kinds of workflows CWER uses day to day. Positron is the successor to RStudio and is what new team members should install.
Key features relevant to CWER work:
Positron also offers AI support, but it does so by connecting to a model of your choice via an API. That is, you need to connect it to a model via your AI model account (e.g., Claude). This approach also charges a small amount per prompt. To avoid these charges, you can do all of your AI prompting in your Clarity or your Claude account and paste code into the Positron console. Alternatively, if you are relying heavily on AI support, Cursor may feel more natural (see below).
Download Positron at positron.posit.co.

title: 6  IDEs
text:
R console — run R code interactively, see output and plots inline
Variable explorer — inspect objects in your R environment at a glance
Quarto support — write, preview, and render .qmd documents natively
Git integration — stage, commit, push, and pull from the Source Control panel without opening the terminal
Terminal — access the command line directly within the application
Extension support — install extensions to add functionality (see the Tools Overview chapter for recommended extensions)






The Positron interface showing the Explorer panel, editor, console, and plots panel.



6.1.1 Getting Started in Positron
Positron works by opening a folder as your workspace rather than individual files. When you start working on a project, use File → Open Folder to open the project’s root directory. All of Positron’s tools — the file explorer, R console, terminal, and Git panel — will then work relative to that folder.
The main panels you will use:

Explorer (left sidebar, top icon) — browse and open files in your project
Source Control (left sidebar, branching icon) — Git operations: stage, commit, push, pull
Console (bottom panel) — run R code interactively
Terminal (bottom panel) — run shell commands
Plots (right panel) — view figures produced by R



6.1.2 Git in Positron
For day-to-day Git operations, the Source Control panel is the most convenient approach:

Make changes to your files
Click the Source Control icon in the left sidebar
Changed files appear under Changes — click the + next to each file to stage it, or click + next to Changes to stage all
Type a commit message in the box at the top
Click the ✓ Commit button
Click the Sync button (or the push icon) to push to GitHub




The Source Control panel in Positron showing staged changes ready to commit.


For pulling changes from teammates, click the Sync button or use the … menu and select Pull.

title: 6  IDEs
text:
6.2 Cursor
Cursor is an IDE built on the same foundation as Positron and VS Code but with deeper AI integration woven throughout the editing experience. Where Positron treats AI as an add-on, Cursor builds AI assistance into the core of how you write code. It can suggest completions, edit code in response to natural language instructions, and answer questions about your codebase without leaving the editor.
Cursor is a good choice for team members who are doing substantial coding work and want AI assistance that feels more seamless than copying and pasting between an IDE and a chat interface. It supports R, Stata (through a Stata MCP extension), works with Quarto documents, and has the same Git integration as Positron.



The Cursor interface showing the Explorer panel, editor, and AI chat panel.



6.2.1 Key Features
Main features that differentiate Cursor from Positron:

Inline AI editing — highlight code and give natural language instructions to edit it in place (e.g., “recode this to use tidyverse” or “add error handling here”)
AI chat panel — a dedicated panel for asking questions about your code and project, accessible without leaving the editor
AI autocomplete — context-aware suggestions that go beyond standard autocomplete, drawing on the surrounding code and your project structure
Model choice — use Claude, GPT-4, or other models depending on the task. Cursor’s default mode switches between AI models based on the task



6.2.2 AI Chat
The AI chat panel is one of Cursor’s most useful features for research workflows. You can ask questions about your code, request explanations of functions, or ask Cursor to help debug an error — all within the editor, with full awareness of your project’s files and context.



The Cursor AI chat panel in action.


To open the chat panel, press Cmd+L or click the chat icon in the right sidebar.


6.2.3 Git in Cursor
Cursor has the same Source Control panel as Positron, accessible via the branching icon in the left sidebar. The workflow is identical — stage files, write a commit message, commit, and push.



The Source Control panel in Cursor showing staged changes ready to commit.


One additional feature: Cursor can generate commit messages automatically based on your staged changes. Click the sparkle icon in the commit message box to generate a message.


6.2.4 Download
Download Cursor at cursor.com. Cursor is free for individual use with generous limits on AI features.

title: 6  IDEs
text:
6.3 Which Should I Use?
The choice is yours. For some CWER team members, starting with Positron may feel more natural, especially if you’ve had experience with RStudio. It is purpose-built for data science, has excellent R and Quarto support, and is straightforward to learn. Once you are comfortable with your analysis workflows, consider trying Cursor if you find yourself frequently switching between your IDE and a chat interface for coding help.
You also don’t have to choose one over the other. Both IDEs are free. You can have both installed and switch between them as they work with the same files and Git repositories.

title: 7  Claude Code
text: Claude Code is a command-line AI coding tool from Anthropic that can assist with writing, editing, and explaining code directly in your terminal. It is particularly useful for larger coding tasks where you want AI assistance without leaving your development environment. Claude Code is optional but recommended for team members doing substantial R or Stata work. See the Getting Started chapter for setup guidance.

title: 8  Working with AI
text:
8.1 About Our AI Tools
This chapter describes how CWER uses AI tools across three areas of research work: idea generation, reviewing and editing, and data management and analysis. Each section includes the prompts and setups we use in practice.
CWER uses Claude (by Anthropic) as its primary AI tool. We chose Claude for two reasons: its strong performance on research and analytical tasks, and Anthropic’s serious attention to the ethical development of AI. Gemini (by Google) is our secondary recommendation for the same reasons — strong performance combined with a developer that takes responsible AI seriously. Both are available through Yale’s Clarity platform (see below).
We do not have a blanket recommendation for other tools, though the landscape is evolving quickly. When in doubt, ask Mike or Joanna before using a new AI tool for anything related to lab work.

title: 8  Working with AI
text:
8.2 Data Security and AI
Yale’s Clarity platform (clarity.yale.edu) is the only platform approved for working with identifiable or sensitive research data. Clarity provides secure, encrypted connections and assures that Yale data will not be used to train AI models. It supports Claude, GPT-4o, and Gemini, and is appropriate for most high-risk data with the exception of electronic Protected Health Information (ePHI). If your work involves ePHI or data use agreements with external partners, check with Mike or Joanna before using any AI tool — including Clarity.
When using Claude.ai, Gemini, or any other external AI tool outside of Clarity, never paste real participant data. Use anonymized or synthetic examples instead.

title: 8  Working with AI
text:
8.3 General Prompting Principles
Getting useful output from AI requires clear, specific input. A few principles that apply across all tasks:
Give context first. AI tools have no memory of your project unless you tell them. Before asking for help, describe what you are working on, what software you are using, what you have already tried, and what the output should look like.
Be specific about what you want. “Help me clean this data” is too vague. “I have a CSV with 342 rows and 45 variables. I need to recode variables coded -99 and -98 to NA, reverse score items 3, 7, and 12, and compute a mean composite score across items 1-10, excluding cases with more than 20% missing. Write R code using tidyverse.” gives the AI something concrete to work with.
Treat output as a first draft. Review every piece of AI-generated code before running it. Check that it does what you intended. AI output can be subtly wrong in ways that do not throw errors — always verify results against expectations.
Use AI as a thought partner, not an oracle. Some of the most valuable uses of AI are not asking it to produce something, but asking it to think alongside you — to surface considerations you may have missed, identify gaps in a plan, or present the argument against an approach you are leaning toward. The measurement development prompt below is a concrete example of this in practice.
Note AI contributions. Document AI assistance in your script headers and, where appropriate, in manuscript methods sections. This reflects our commitment to research transparency described in the Being a Team Member chapter.

title: 8  Working with AI
text:
8.4 Writing Assistance with Grammarly
Grammarly is an AI-powered writing assistant that checks grammar, spelling, punctuation, clarity, and style in real time. The recommended version for CWER team members is the Grammarly desktop app, which works seamlessly across all the applications you already use — Word, Outlook, email clients, web browsers, and desktop applications — without any copying and pasting. Suggestions appear inline wherever you are writing.
For CWER work, Grammarly is most useful for:

Polishing manuscript drafts, reports, and partner communications before sharing
Catching typos and grammatical errors in research reports and grant proposals
Improving sentence clarity and concision in methods and results sections
Checking team member writing during the review process


8.4.1 Setup
Download the Grammarly desktop app for Mac or Windows at grammarly.com/desktop. Installation is straightforward — once installed, Grammarly works automatically in the background across your applications. The free version covers grammar, spelling, and punctuation. Grammarly Pro adds clarity, tone, and style suggestions, which are useful for academic writing.


8.4.2 A Note on Data Privacy
Do not use Grammarly with text containing sensitive research data or participant information. Grammarly processes text on external servers. For sensitive content, use Microsoft Word’s built-in Editor (Review → Editor) instead, which provides grammar and style checking without sending text to external servers. You can also temporarily turn Grammarly off for a specific application using the Grammarly desktop app settings.

title: 8  Working with AI
text:
8.5 Idea Generation
AI is well-suited for early-stage thinking — exploring a literature you are less familiar with, stress-testing a research design, or identifying considerations you may have overlooked. The key is to treat AI output as a starting point that you verify and refine, not as a definitive answer.

8.5.1 Effect Size Interpretation
When evaluating education research involving effect sizes, we use guidance from Kraft (2019), which provides empirically grounded benchmarks based on 1,942 effect sizes from 747 RCTs of education interventions. Cohen’s conventional benchmarks (small = 0.2, medium = 0.5, large = 0.8) were derived from small lab studies and are far too large relative to what field-based education interventions actually produce. Kraft’s adjusted benchmarks are:



Category
Threshold




Small
< 0.05 SD


Medium
0.05–0.19 SD


Large
≥ 0.20 SD



The median effect size across education RCTs is 0.10 SD. Large-scale, pre-registered RCTs produce medians as low as 0.03 SD.
When asking AI to help interpret or contextualize effect sizes, include this framing in your prompt so the AI applies these benchmarks rather than Cohen’s conventions.
System prompt for effect size work (use in a Claude Project or paste at the start of a conversation):
When evaluating education-related research involving effect sizes, 
use Kraft (2019) benchmarks rather than Cohen's conventions: 
small < 0.05 SD, medium 0.05–0.19 SD, large ≥ 0.20 SD. The median 
effect size across education RCTs is 0.10 SD. Note the following 
when contextualizing effects: (1) whether the design is causal or 
correlational, (2) whether the outcome is proximal or distal to the 
intervention, (3) design features that may inflate effect sizes 
(small/targeted samples, researcher-designed measures, TOT vs. ITT), 
and (4) cost and scalability considerations.
Interpreting an effect size:
I am reviewing a study of an SEL intervention that reports a 
Cohen's d of 0.18 on a teacher-reported SEL measure administered 
immediately post-intervention. The study used a randomized design 
with N=280 students in grades 3-5. Using Kraft (2019) benchmarks, 
how should I interpret this effect size? What contextual factors 
are relevant?
Exploring a literature:
I am designing a study on [topic]. Before I finalize the design, 
what are the key bodies of empirical literature I should be 
familiar with? What are the main debates or unresolved questions 
in this area? Please be specific about subfields and landmark 
papers where possible — I will verify and read the primary sources.
Stress-testing a research design:
Here is my proposed research design: [describe design]. Before 
I finalize it, what are the strongest methodological objections 
someone could raise? What threats to validity am I most exposed 
to? What would a skeptical reviewer say about this approach?


8.5.2 Measurement Development
For measurement development work, we use a dedicated Claude Project (or Cowork session) with a system prompt and an attached reference document. This keeps the AI grounded in established psychometric evidence rather than generating advice from general knowledge alone.
The reference document is scale_best_practices.docx, which lives in the lab’s shared Google Drive. This document was built from primary scientific literature by psychometric experts — Krosnick & Presser (2010), Preston & Colman (2000), Saris et al. (2010), Schaeffer & Dykema (2020), Ward & Meade (2023), and others. The document was not generated by AI. AI was used as a thought partner during its development — asked to identify bodies of work and considerations that might be missing — but all editorial decisions about what to include were made by Mike. The result is a reference grounded in the actual scientific literature, not in AI’s general impressions of measurement best practices.
This is a concrete example of the thought partner approach: you drive the intellectual work, use AI to surface potential blind spots, and exercise your own judgment about what belongs. The AI does not write the document — it helps you ask better questions about what should be in it.
System prompt for the Claude Project:
This involves measurement issues related to other research projects. 
Take the role of an expert in measurement development. Use the 
document on best practices to guide but feel free to go beyond this.
Attach scale_best_practices.docx to the project before beginning.
Designing a new scale:
I am developing a teacher-report measure of student self-management 
for use in grades K-3. The construct includes behavioral regulation, 
emotional control, and goal-directed persistence. I plan to use a 
5-point response scale. Based on the best practices document, what 
response format and label set would you recommend? Are there any 
considerations specific to teacher-report measures I should be 
aware of?
Reviewing existing items:
Here are 8 draft items for a social awareness subscale. Please 
review them against the best practices document and flag any 
concerns about item wording, response format match, potential 
acquiescence bias, or cognitive burden. Suggest revisions where 
needed.

[paste items here]
Using AI as a thought partner on scale design:
I have made the following decisions about my scale design: 
[describe decisions]. Before I finalize, what considerations or 
tradeoffs might I be overlooking? Are there bodies of psychometric 
literature I should consult that the best practices document does 
not cover?

title: 8  Working with AI
text:
8.6 Reviewing & Editing
When asking AI to review written work, the most useful output comes from prompts that specify the review criteria clearly and explicitly discourage flattery. The following system prompt is used for reviewing research writing in CWER. It can be pasted at the start of a conversation or set as a system prompt in a Claude Project.
System prompt for reviewing research writing:
Content in here concerns research projects.

When I ask you to review something I've written, check to make 
sure of the following:
1. Text is in APA format unless requested otherwise.
2. The writing is clear, as free of jargon as possible, and concise.
3. Logical arguments are sound.
4. There are no statements that are weakly argued or lack 
   justification. Arguments should not overstate or understate 
   the evidence.
5. If I'm critiquing someone's work, make sure I'm doing so 
   charitably and accurately.

Do not flatter me when reviewing my work. Be constructively 
critical and put yourself in the role of a reviewer at a 
scientific journal who is aiming to improve the work through 
strong critique.
Reviewing a manuscript section:
Please review the following [methods / results / discussion] 
section using the criteria in your instructions. Identify the 
most important issues first, then secondary issues. Be specific 
about what to fix and why.

[paste text here]
Verifying a reference:
Please verify that the following reference is real and provide 
a correctly formatted APA 7th edition citation and a sample 
parenthetical in-text citation I can use directly in a manuscript.

[paste reference here]
Checking for misreporting:
Here are my analysis results and the corresponding text from my 
results section. Please check for any discrepancies between the 
numbers in the text and the numbers in the output — wrong values, 
rounding errors, or results described in the wrong direction.

Results output: [paste output]
Results text: [paste text]
Drafting a methods section:
I ran a multilevel model in R using lme4. Here are the details: 
[describe model]. Write a methods section paragraph describing 
the analytic approach in APA style, appropriate for a journal 
manuscript. Do not invent any details — only use what I have 
provided.

title: 8  Working with AI
text:
8.7 Data Management and Analysis
AI is an invaluable tool for speeding up data management and analysis tasks. See the Data Management and Statistical Analysis sections for examples of prompts.

title: 9  R
text:
9.1 Installing R
R is a free, open-source programming language designed for statistical computing and data analysis. Unlike point-and-click software like SPSS, R requires you to write code — instructions that tell the software exactly what to do with your data. This has a steeper learning curve at first, but pays off quickly: R code is reproducible, shareable, and far more flexible than menu-driven software.
R works through packages — collections of functions written by the research community that extend what R can do. Base R comes with a set of built-in functions, and packages add specialized capabilities on top of that.
Download and install R from cran.r-project.org. For the IDE to write and run R code, see the IDEs chapter.

title: 9  R
text:
9.2 R Basics

9.2.1 The Console and Scripts
When you open a project in Positron, you will see a Console panel at the bottom. This is where R runs. You can type commands directly into the console and press Enter to run them:
2 + 2
However, for any real analysis you should write code in a script file rather than typing directly into the console. In CWER we use Quarto documents (.qmd) as our script files — these combine R code and written explanation in a single document. See the Quarto section below for more.


9.2.2 Objects and Assignment
R stores information in objects. You create an object by assigning a value to a name using <-:
my_number <- 42
my_name <- "Michael"
Once created, you can use the object by name:
my_number * 2
Object names should follow the same snake_case conventions described in the Data Management chapter.


9.2.3 Functions
R does things through functions. A function takes inputs (called arguments), does something with them, and returns an output:
mean(c(1, 2, 3, 4, 5))
Here mean() is the function and c(1, 2, 3, 4, 5) is the argument — a vector of five numbers. The function returns their mean: 3.
You will spend most of your time in R calling functions from packages. The pattern is always the same: function_name(argument1, argument2, ...).


9.2.4 Getting Help
To see the documentation for any function, type ? followed by the function name:
?mean


9.2.5 Installing and Loading Packages
Install a package once using install.packages():
install.packages("tidyverse")
Load a package at the start of each session using library():
library(tidyverse)
You only need to install a package once, but you need to load it with library() every time you start a new R session. In CWER projects that use renv, package installation is managed automatically — see the renv section below.

title: 9  R
text:
9.3 Quarto for Reporting
Quarto documents (.qmd) are the standard format for analysis scripts and reports in CWER. A Quarto document weaves together R code, output, and written narrative in a single file. When you render a Quarto document, it runs all the code and produces a polished HTML or Word document with results embedded.
This means your analysis and your report are the same document — there is no copying and pasting results from R into Word, which eliminates a major source of reporting errors.

9.3.1 Document Structure
A Quarto document has three parts:
1. YAML header — document metadata at the top, surrounded by ---:
---
title: "Wave 1 Descriptive Analysis"
author: "Your Name"
date: today
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    code-fold: true
    embed-resources: true
status: development
---
2. Code chunks — R code with chunk options prefixed by #|:
#| label: setup
#| message: false
#| warning: false

library(tidyverse)
library(here)

data <- read_csv(here("data", "cleaned", "wave1_cleaned.csv"))
3. Narrative text — written explanation using Markdown formatting between code chunks.


9.3.2 Code Chunk Options



Option
Values
Effect




label
any text
Names the chunk — required for figures


echo
true/false
Show or hide the code


warning
true/false
Show or hide warnings


message
true/false
Show or hide messages


eval
true/false
Run or skip the chunk


fig-width
number
Figure width in inches


fig-height
number
Figure height in inches


fig-cap
text
Figure caption





9.3.3 The Setup Chunk
Every Quarto document should begin with a setup chunk that loads packages, reads data, and recodes missing values:
#| label: setup
#| message: false
#| warning: false

# ============================================================
# Script:  02_descriptives.qmd
# Author:  Your Name (your.email@yale.edu)
# Created: 2025-10-01
# Purpose: Descriptive statistics for Wave 1 survey data
# Inputs:  data/cleaned/wave1_cleaned.csv
# Outputs: output/tables/descriptives_table.docx
# AI:      Code developed with Claude assistance
# Review:  Reviewed by Michael Strambler, 2025-10-05
# ============================================================

library(tidyverse)
library(here)

data <- read_csv(here("data", "cleaned", "wave1_cleaned.csv"))

data <- data |>
  mutate(across(everything(), ~ if_else(. %in% c(-99, -98, -97, -96), NA, .)))


9.3.4 Inline Code
Embed R results directly in narrative text:
The sample included `r nrow(data)` students across 
`r n_distinct(data$school_id)` schools.
When rendered, this inserts the actual values — eliminating manual updating of numbers when data changes.


9.3.5 Rendering
Render from the Positron Terminal:
quarto render code/02_descriptives.qmd
Or click the Render button at the top of the file in Positron.


9.3.6 Output Formats for CWER



Use case
Recommended format




Internal analysis and review
HTML with embed-resources: true


Sharing with partners or stakeholders
Word (.docx)


Publication supplementary materials
HTML or PDF

title: 9  R
text:
9.4 Managing Packages with renv
renv manages R package versions at the project level. Without it, R installs packages into a single system-wide library shared across all your projects — meaning updating a package for one project can silently break another. renv gives each project its own isolated package library and records exact versions in a lockfile.

9.4.1 Setting Up renv
With your project open in Positron, run in the R console:
install.packages("renv")
renv::init()
renv scans your project for packages, installs them into a project-specific library, and creates:

renv.lock — records the exact version of every package
.Rprofile — automatically activates renv when R starts in this project

When renv is active, you will see this message at the start of every R session:
- Project '~/Documents/GitHub/my-project' loaded. [renv 1.2.0]


9.4.2 Installing New Packages
install.packages("lme4")
renv::snapshot()
Always snapshot after installing new packages, then commit renv.lock to GitHub.


9.4.3 Restoring Packages
When a teammate clones the project:
renv::restore()
This installs exactly the versions recorded in renv.lock.


9.4.4 Checking Status
renv::status()


9.4.5 What Gets Committed to GitHub



File / Folder
Commit?




renv.lock
Yes — essential for reproducibility


.Rprofile
Yes — activates renv automatically


renv/library/
No — large, can be restored from lockfile

title: 9  R
text:
9.5 CWER R Conventions

Use snake_case for all object and variable names
Always use here() for file paths — never hardcode absolute paths
Load all packages at the top of each script in the setup chunk
Recode CWER missing data codes (-99, -98, -97, -96) to NA before any analysis
Never overwrite raw data — read from data/raw/, write to data/cleaned/
Use renv for all projects and commit renv.lock to GitHub
Include a script header with author, purpose, inputs, outputs, AI use, and review status

title: 10  Mplus
text:
10.1 How Mplus Works
Mplus is a statistical software package designed for latent variable modeling. It handles a wide range of analyses that are difficult or impossible in general-purpose software like R or Stata — confirmatory factor analysis, structural equation modeling, multilevel modeling with latent variables, mixture modeling, and more. CWER uses Mplus primarily for psychometric work (CFA, EFA, IRT) and for analyses that involve latent variables, missing data via FIML, or complex model structures.
Mplus is syntax-driven — you write a plain text input file (.inp) that specifies your data, variables, model, and output options. Mplus reads this file, runs the analysis, and writes results to an output file (.out). There is no point-and-click interface.
A Mplus input file has a standard structure with named sections:
Each section ends when the next one begins. Always follow this order.

title: 10  Mplus
text:
TITLE:    A description of the analysis
DATA:     Where the data file is
VARIABLE: Variable names and analysis options
ANALYSIS: Estimator and model options
MODEL:    The model specification
OUTPUT:   What output to request

title: 10  Mplus
section: 10.2 The CWER Workflow: Mplus via MplusAutomation
text:
10.2 The CWER Workflow: Mplus via MplusAutomation
Rather than running Mplus directly and manually transferring results, CWER uses MplusAutomation — an R package that lets you prepare data, run Mplus models, and read results back into R without leaving your analysis environment. This keeps the entire workflow reproducible and within a single script.
The general workflow is:

Prepare data in R — clean, recode, and rename variables to Mplus-compatible names (8 characters or fewer)
Write the .dat file — use prepareMplusData() to export data in Mplus format
Write the .inp file — write your Mplus syntax stored in code/mplus/
Run the model — use runModels() to call Mplus from R
Read results — use readModels() to import output back into R
Report — extract fit indices, parameter estimates, and other results for tables and figures


10.2.1 Installing MplusAutomation
install.packages("MplusAutomation")
library(MplusAutomation)
Mplus must be installed on your computer for MplusAutomation to work. It calls Mplus in the background.


10.2.2 Preparing Data
prepareMplusData() writes an R dataframe to a Mplus-compatible .dat file:
prepareMplusData(
  data_mplus,
  filename = here("code", "mplus", "wave1_data.dat")
)


10.2.3 Variable Names in Mplus
Mplus enforces an 8-character limit on variable names. Rename your full snake_case variable names to short Mplus-compatible names before calling prepareMplusData() and document the mapping in your script header:
data_mplus <- data |>
  rename(
    selsm1 = sel_selfmgmt_1,
    selsm2 = sel_selfmgmt_2,
    selsa1 = sel_socaware_1,
    schlid = school_id,
    stuid  = student_id
  )


10.2.4 Running Models
# Run a single model
runModels(here("code", "mplus", "cfa_model.inp"))

# Run all models in a folder
runModels(here("code", "mplus"))


10.2.5 Reading Results
# Read a single output file
output <- readModels(here("code", "mplus", "cfa_model.out"))

# Read all output files in a folder
all_output <- readModels(here("code", "mplus"), recursive = FALSE)

# Key elements of the output object
output$summaries                        # Fit indices
output$parameters$unstandardized        # Unstandardized estimates
output$parameters$stdyx.standardized    # Standardized estimates
output$parameters$indirect              # Indirect effects


10.2.6 A Complete MplusAutomation Workflow
library(tidyverse)
library(here)
library(MplusAutomation)

# 1. Load cleaned data
data <- readRDS(here("data", "cleaned", "wave1_cleaned.rds"))

# 2. Rename variables to Mplus-compatible names
# Mapping: sel_selfmgmt_1 -> selsm1, school_id -> schlid
data_mplus <- data |>
  rename(
    selsm1 = sel_selfmgmt_1,
    selsm2 = sel_selfmgmt_2,
    selsm3 = sel_selfmgmt_3,
    selsa1 = sel_socaware_1,
    selsa2 = sel_socaware_2,
    schlid = school_id,
    stuid  = student_id
  )

# 3. Write Mplus data file
prepareMplusData(
  data_mplus,
  filename = here("code", "mplus", "wave1_data.dat")
)

# 4. Run model
runModels(here("code", "mplus", "cfa_model.inp"))

# 5. Read results
output <- readModels(here("code", "mplus", "cfa_model.out"))

# 6. Extract and save fit indices
fit <- output$summaries |>
  select(CFI, TLI, RMSEA_Estimate, RMSEA_90CI_LB, RMSEA_90CI_UB, SRMR)

write_csv(fit, here("output", "tables", "cfa_fit.csv"))

# 7. Extract and save standardized loadings
loadings <- output$parameters$stdyx.standardized |>
  filter(grepl("BY", paramHeader))

write_csv(loadings, here("output", "tables", "cfa_loadings.csv"))

title: 10  Mplus
text:
10.3 File Organization
Store all Mplus-related files in a dedicated code/mplus/ subfolder:
code/
├── 02_cfa_analysis.qmd        # R script using MplusAutomation
└── mplus/
    ├── wave1_data.dat         # Data file written by prepareMplusData()
    ├── cfa_model.inp          # Mplus input syntax
    ├── cfa_model.out          # Mplus output (generated automatically)
    └── cfa_model.gh5          # Mplus graph file (generated automatically)
Commit .inp files to GitHub — they are your analysis syntax and should be version controlled. Do not commit .dat files if they contain sensitive participant data.

title: 10  Mplus
text:
10.4 Missing Data
Declare your missing data codes in the VARIABLE section:
VARIABLE:
  MISSING = ALL(-99 -98 -97 -96);
Mplus handles missing data via Full Information Maximum Likelihood (FIML) by default when using the ML estimator. FIML uses all available data to estimate parameters without imputation, which is generally preferred over listwise deletion.

title: 10  Mplus
text:
10.5 Estimators



Outcome Type
Recommended Estimator




Continuous, normally distributed
ML


Continuous, non-normal
MLR (robust ML)


Ordered categorical (Likert)
WLSMV


Binary
WLSMV



MLR is a good default for most CWER work with continuous outcomes. WLSMV is the standard choice for ordinal data such as Likert-scale items.

title: 10  Mplus
text:
10.6 Fit Index Guidelines



Index
Acceptable
Good




CFI
≥ .90
≥ .95


TLI
≥ .90
≥ .95


RMSEA
≤ .08
≤ .06


SRMR
≤ .10
≤ .08



These are guidelines, not hard cutoffs. Always interpret fit indices in context and report multiple indices.

title: 11  Project Organization
text:
11.1 Standard Organization
A well-organized project should answer basic questions through its structure alone. Where did the data come from? Which script produced which output? Is this analysis current? And it should be able to do this without requiring anyone to read code or ask someone who was there. This chapter describes the folder conventions we use across CWER projects. Once internalized, setting up a new project takes only a few minutes, but the payoff in clarity and reproducibility is substantial.
We use two types of organizational structures. One is for standard non-coding files. This is for files like literature, manuscripts, grant proposals, and so on. This will be the most commonly accessed structure. The other structure is for technical files that involve coding and data analysis.
Use the following conventions for all non-coding folders and files stored on OneDrive or other shared drives.

title: 11  Project Organization
text:
Input files are fixed. Data that comes from outside the project such as partner-provided files, downloaded datasets, or exported survey data, goes in data/raw/ and is never modified by your scripts. If you need a cleaned version, your script reads the original, transforms it, and saves the result to data/cleaned/.
Outputs are reproducible. Everything your scripts produce such as figures, tables, and rendered reports goes in output/ and can always be regenerated by rerunning the script that created it. Because of this, they are not committed to GitHub.

title: 11  Project Organization
text:
11.2 Standard Project Structure
A typical CWER project looks like this:
my-project/
├── data/                   # All data files
│   ├── raw/                # Original inputs — scripts never modify these
│   ├── cleaned/            # Processed data produced by cleaning scripts
│   └── README.md           # Documents where each raw file came from
├── code/                   # Analysis scripts (.qmd, .do, .R, .inp)
│   └── exploratory/        # One-off analyses and sanity checks
├── output/                 # All generated outputs
│   ├── tables/             # Tables (.docx, .xlsx, .csv)
│   └── figures/            # Figures (.png, .pdf)
├── docs/                   # Reports, presentations, manuscripts
└── README.md               # Project overview



The CWER project folder structure as it appears in the Positron Explorer panel.


Not every project needs every subfolder. Adapt as needed, but keep the core separation between data/raw/ and everything else intact.

title: 11  Project Organization
text:
11.3 The data/ Folder

11.3.1 data/raw/
The data/raw/ folder holds files that come from outside the project — things your scripts read but never modify. This includes:

Survey data exported from Qualtrics or a partner system
Data received from school districts or community partners
Publicly available datasets downloaded from external sources
Codebooks and metadata provided by partners

Treat everything in data/raw/ as read-only. Scripts read from it but never write to it. If a raw file needs to be corrected, replace it and document the change in data/README.md. Do not silently overwrite it.


11.3.2 data/cleaned/
The data/cleaned/ folder holds processed versions of raw data produced by your cleaning scripts. These are intermediate files that other scripts will use as inputs, such as merged datasets, recoded variables, reshaped files, and so on. Because cleaned files are generated by code, they can always be recreated by rerunning the cleaning script.


11.3.3 data/README.md
Always include a data/README.md that documents where each raw file came from, when it was received, and any relevant notes. This takes two minutes to write and is invaluable six months later when you are trying to remember which version of a dataset came from which partner, or which Qualtrics export corresponds to which wave of data collection.
Example data/README.md:
# Data Sources

## raw/care4kids_survey_wave1.csv
- Source: CT Office of Early Childhood, received from Jane Smith via Yale OneDrive File Transfer
- Received: 2025-09-15
- Notes: Wave 1 only (fall 2025). N=342. Includes provider ID but no names.

## raw/sel_codebook_v3.xlsx
- Source: Internal; developed by CWER team
- Last updated: 2025-08-01
- Notes: Version 3 adds items from the revised teacher observation protocol

title: 11  Project Organization
text:
11.4 The code/ Folder
Analysis scripts are numbered to reflect the order of the pipeline:
code/
├── 01_data_cleaning.qmd
├── 02_descriptives.qmd
├── 03_mlm_analysis.qmd
├── 04_tables_figures.qmd
└── exploratory/
The two-digit prefix means scripts appear in pipeline order when you list the folder. Script 01 reads from data/raw/ and writes cleaned files to data/cleaned/. Script 02 reads from data/cleaned/ and produces descriptives. A new team member can look at the file list and understand the overall flow without reading any code.
Numbers reflect logical order, not strict dependencies. Script 04 might read outputs from both scripts 01 and 03. The numbering just helps you understand the overall structure.
When you add a new script, assign the next available number. If you retire a script, do not renumber the others. Leave gaps rather than creating confusion about which script a particular output came from.

11.4.1 Script Headers
Every script should begin with a header block identifying the author, purpose, inputs, and outputs:
# ============================================================
# Script:  01_data_cleaning.qmd
# Author:  Your Name (your.email@yale.edu)
# Created: 2025-10-01
# Purpose: Clean and merge Wave 1 survey data
# Inputs:  data/raw/care4kids_survey_wave1.csv
# Outputs: data/cleaned/wave1_cleaned.rds
# AI:      Code developed with Claude assistance
# Review:  Reviewed by Michael Strambler, 2025-10-05
# ============================================================
The AI and Review lines reflect our commitments to transparency and quality control described in the Statistical Analysis chapter.


11.4.2 Script Status
Add a status field to each script’s YAML header to signal where it is in its lifecycle:
---
title: "Multilevel Model Analysis"
author: "Your Name"
date: today
status: development
---







Status
Meaning




development
In active development; outputs may change


finalized
Outputs are publication-ready; changes should be deliberate


deprecated
Superseded by a newer script; kept for reference



When results are heading toward a report or publication, mark the relevant scripts finalized. This signals to collaborators that any changes should be intentional and that outputs should be re-verified if the script is rerun.


11.4.3 Exploratory Scripts
Think of the code/exploratory/ subfolder as a playground for for one-off analyses, sanity checks, and ideas you are testing. Exploratory scripts follow relaxed rules with no number prefixes required, and nothing else in the project depends on their outputs. This makes it safe to experiment without worrying about breaking the main pipeline. If an exploratory analysis turns out to be valuable, promote it to a numbered script in the main directory.

title: 11  Project Organization
text:
11.5 The output/ Folder
All generated outputs go in output/, organized into two subfolders:
output/
├── tables/
│   ├── descriptives_table.docx
│   └── mlm_results_table.docx
└── figures/
    ├── figure1_histogram.png
    └── figure2_scatter.pdf
Keep file names descriptive so their purpose is clear without opening them. Avoid generic names like table1.docx or figure_final.png.

title: 11  Project Organization
text:
11.6 The docs/ Folder
The docs/ folder holds reports, presentations, manuscripts, and other documents intended for external audiences or stakeholders. These are the human-readable products of the project — the things you send to partners, funders, or journals. Unlike output/, which holds raw generated files, docs/ holds polished, finalized deliverables.

title: 11  Project Organization
text:
11.7 Cross-Software Data Format
When data produced by one software needs to be read by another — for example, R producing a dataset that Mplus will read, or Stata producing a file that R will analyze — save in a format both can read:

CSV is the most universally accessible format and the default choice for sharing data across R, Stata, Mplus, and SPSS
Excel (.xlsx) is acceptable when the recipient is more comfortable with spreadsheets, but be aware that formatting and formulas can cause problems when read programmatically
When saving from R for Mplus input, use MplusAutomation package functions to ensure the format is correct

Within a single software environment, use native formats where appropriate (.rds for R objects, .dta for Stata) to preserve data types and attributes.

title: 12  Data Management
text:
12.1 Codebooks
Good data management is the foundation of reproducible research. When data are well-organized, clearly documented, and consistently formatted, analyses are easier to run, errors are easier to catch, and handing off work to a teammate is straightforward. This chapter describes CWER conventions for codebooks, variable naming, and missing data. These conventions apply across all projects and all software platforms.
A codebook is the authoritative record of every variable in a dataset. It documents what each variable measures, how it was collected, what the response options mean, and how missing data are coded. Every CWER project that involves original data collection must have a codebook.

title: 12  Data Management
text:
12.1.1 Format
Codebooks are maintained as Excel files (.xlsx) stored in the docs/ folder of the project. Use one sheet per instrument or data source. The file should be named descriptively, for example codebook_care4kids_wave1.xlsx.


12.1.2 Required Columns
Every codebook must include the following columns:







Column
Description




variable_name
The exact variable name as it appears in the dataset


variable_label
A plain-language description of what the variable measures


source
Where the variable came from (e.g., survey item, administrative record, derived)


item_text
The exact question or item wording presented to respondents, if applicable


response_values
The numeric values used to represent response categories


response_labels
The text label for each numeric value


missing_codes
Any special codes used to represent missing data for this variable


notes
Any additional information relevant to using or interpreting this variable





12.1.3 Example Row













variable_name
variable_label
source
item_text
response_values
response_labels
missing_codes
notes




sel_selfmgmt_1
Self-management item 1
Teacher survey
“This student controls their emotions”
1, 2, 3, 4
Never, Sometimes, Often, Always
-99, -98
Reverse coded for composite





12.1.4 Keeping Codebooks Current
A codebook is only useful if it is kept up to date. Update it whenever:

A new variable is added to the dataset
A variable is recoded or transformed
A response category changes meaning across waves
A missing data code changes

Treat the codebook as part of the analysis, not an afterthought. Ideally, draft the codebook before data collection begins — this forces clarity about what you are measuring and how you will analyze it.

title: 12  Data Management
text:
12.2 File and Folder Naming Conventions
Consistent naming makes projects easier to navigate, reduces errors, and ensures files work correctly across different operating systems and software. CWER follows two conventions depending on context:

Dashes for file and folder names — dashes are URL-friendly and the standard for web-facing files. When Quarto renders documents, file names become URLs, and dashes produce cleaner, more readable links than underscores.
Underscores for variable names — snake_case is the standard for R, Stata, and data science broadly. See the Variable Naming Conventions section above.




Context
Convention
Example




File names
kebab-case (dashes)
data-wrangling.qmd


Folder names
kebab-case (dashes)
cwer-docs/


Variable names
snake_case (underscores)
sel_total_mean


R object names
snake_case (underscores)
data_cleaned


Data files
snake_case (underscores)
wave1_cleaned.rds



Note that data files use underscores rather than dashes. This is because data files are read programmatically by R, Stata, and SPSS, where underscores are safer and more conventional than dashes.

12.2.1 Rules for All File and Folder Names

Use lowercase only. wave1-cleaned.rds, not Wave1-Cleaned.rds
Be descriptive. wave1-provider-survey.csv tells you what the file is; data.csv does not
Avoid spaces. Spaces in file names cause problems in terminal commands and some software. Use dashes or underscores instead
Avoid special characters. No parentheses, slashes, ampersands, or symbols of any kind
No date prefixes on individual files. Do not use names like analysis-2025-10-01.qmd — let Git track versions instead. If you genuinely need multiple dated versions to coexist (e.g., comparing model runs), use a dated subfolder inside output/ rather than dating the file itself



12.2.2 Rules for Script Files
Number scripts to reflect pipeline order:
code/
├── 01-data-cleaning.qmd
├── 02-descriptives.qmd
├── 03-mlm-analysis.qmd
└── 04-tables-figures.qmd
The two-digit prefix ensures scripts appear in order when listed. When you add a new script, use the next available number. If you retire a script, do not renumber the remaining ones — leave gaps rather than creating confusion about which outputs correspond to which script.


12.2.3 Rules for Output Files
Name output files to reflect their content and the script that produced them:

output/figures/sel-by-grade-boxplot.png — descriptive, clear
output/tables/wave1-mlm-results.docx — clear what analysis and wave
Not: output/figures/figure-final2.png — tells you nothing

When a script produces multiple outputs of the same type, use a consistent naming pattern:
output/figures/
├── fig1-sel-distribution.png
├── fig2-sel-by-grade.png
└── fig3-sel-by-school.png

title: 12  Data Management
text:
12.3 Variable Naming Conventions
Consistent variable names make datasets easier to work with across software platforms, reduce errors, and make analysis code more readable. The following conventions apply to all CWER datasets.

12.3.1 Why Snake_case?
CWER uses snake_case — lowercase letters with underscores between words — as the standard naming convention. For example: sel_selfmgmt_1, teacher_id, rdg_score_z.
This is a deliberate choice over alternatives like camelCase (SelSelfmgmt1) or PascalCase (SelSelfMgmt1) for several reasons:

Cross-software portability. Snake_case works cleanly across R, Stata, SPSS, and CSV files. Stata is case-sensitive, which means RaceEth and raceeth are treated as different variables — a common source of hard-to-find errors. Lowercase names eliminate this risk entirely.
Readability. sel_selfmgmt_mean is easier to parse at a glance than SelSelfmgmtMean, especially for longer names with multiple components.
Industry standard. Snake_case is the dominant convention in data science — it is what R’s tidyverse, Python, and most database systems use. RAs with any quantitative background will already be familiar with it.



12.3.2 General Rules

Use lowercase letters only. sel_score, not SEL_Score or Sel_Score.
Use underscores to separate words. teacher_rating, not teacherrating or teacher.rating.
Keep names short but meaningful. Aim for 8 characters or fewer when possible for compatibility with older software, but never sacrifice clarity for brevity. A name like rdg_score is better than rs and better than reading_comprehension_total_score.
Avoid spaces and special characters. No spaces, hyphens, slashes, parentheses, or symbols of any kind.
Never start a variable name with a number. wave1_n is valid; 1_wave_n is not.



12.3.3 Naming Structure
For survey and scale items, use the following structure:
[construct]_[subscale]_[item number]
Examples: - sel_selfmgmt_1 — SEL scale, self-management subscale, item 1 - sel_selfmgmt_2 — SEL scale, self-management subscale, item 2 - sel_socaware_1 — SEL scale, social awareness subscale, item 1
For composite scores and derived variables:
[construct]_[subscale]_[statistic or type]
Examples: - sel_selfmgmt_mean — mean of self-management items - sel_total_mean — mean across all SEL items - rdg_pre_score — reading pre-test score - rdg_post_score — reading post-test score
For demographic and administrative variables, use clear, plain names:

age_years — age in years
grade — grade level
gender — gender
race_eth — race/ethnicity
school_id — school identifier
teacher_id — teacher identifier
student_id — student identifier



12.3.4 ID Variables
Every dataset must include at least one ID variable that uniquely identifies each row. Follow these rules for ID variables:

Name ID variables with the unit of observation followed by _id: student_id, teacher_id, school_id, provider_id
ID values should be numeric where possible
Never use names, initials, birthdates, or other personally identifiable information as ID values
Document the ID scheme in the codebook, including how IDs were assigned and whether they are consistent across waves or datasets



12.3.5 Wave and Time Variables
For longitudinal projects, include a wave or time indicator:

wave — numeric wave indicator (1, 2, 3)
time — numeric time point
year — academic or calendar year
season — fall, spring (use text labels)

When variable names need to distinguish across waves, append the wave to the end:

sel_total_mean_w1 — SEL total mean, Wave 1
sel_total_mean_w2 — SEL total mean, Wave 2



12.3.6 Recoded and Derived Variables
When you recode or derive a variable from an existing one, create a new variable rather than overwriting the original. Append a suffix to indicate the transformation:



Suffix
Meaning
Example




_r
Reverse coded
sel_item3_r


_c
Centered
age_years_c


_z
Standardized (z-score)
rdg_score_z


_log
Log transformed
income_log


_cat
Categorical version of a continuous variable
age_cat


_imp
Imputed
rdg_score_imp



Never overwrite raw variables. Always create a new variable and document it in the codebook.


12.3.7 Variable Names in Mplus
Mplus imposes an 8-character limit on variable names, which creates a tension with snake_case. An underscore uses one of those 8 characters, leaving only 7 for meaningful content. For example, sel_sm_1 hits the limit exactly, while longer names like sel_selfmgmt_1 (14 characters) are simply not valid in Mplus syntax.
The recommended approach is to maintain full snake_case names in your master datasets and remap to short Mplus-compatible names only when preparing Mplus input files. The MplusAutomation package in R makes this straightforward — you can keep descriptive names in R, write a short name mapping when exporting to Mplus, and map results back to full names when importing output. This way your master data and R code stay readable, and your Mplus syntax stays within the character limit.
When constructing short Mplus names, follow these guidelines:

Drop underscores and abbreviate: sel_selfmgmt_1 becomes selsm1
Keep names as interpretable as possible within the limit
Document the mapping between full names and Mplus names in your codebook or script header

title: 12  Data Management
text:
12.4 Missing Data Codes
Missing data arise for different reasons, and distinguishing between them is important for accurate analysis and documentation. CWER uses a two-step approach: numeric codes for storage, and software-native formats for analysis.

12.4.1 Step 1: Storage Codes
Use the following numeric codes in all raw and cleaned data files across all projects. These codes travel across software platforms — a CSV file with -99 is readable by R, Stata, SPSS, and Mplus alike.







Code
Meaning




-99
Item not answered / respondent skipped


-98
Not applicable (item did not apply to this respondent)


-97
Data not available (data exist but were not collected or could not be obtained)


-96
Removed during data cleaning (e.g., identified as invalid response)



These codes should be applied consistently within a project and documented in the codebook for each variable that uses them.
Never use 0 to represent missing data unless 0 is a meaningful response value that is completely distinct from missingness — and even then, use caution and document it explicitly.


12.4.2 Step 2: Analysis-Ready Formats
Before running any analysis, recode CWER storage codes to each software’s native missing data format. This is a required step — failing to recode is one of the most common sources of errors in quantitative research. A -99 included in a mean calculation will silently produce a wrong answer.
R — recode to NA:
data <- data |>
  mutate(across(everything(), ~ if_else(. %in% c(-99, -98, -97, -96), NA, .)))
Stata — recode to .:
foreach var of varlist _all {
  recode `var' (-99 -98 -97 -96 = .)
}
Mplus — declare in the MISSING statement:
VARIABLE:
  MISSING = ALL(-99 -98 -97 -96);
SPSS — declare as user-missing values:
MISSING VALUES ALL (-99, -98, -97, -96).
Do this recoding step in your cleaning script (code/01-data-cleaning.qmd or equivalent) so that all downstream analysis scripts work with properly coded missing data from the start.


12.4.3 Documenting Missingness
For every dataset, produce a missing data summary as part of your cleaning script. At minimum, report the number and percentage of missing values for each variable. Flag any variables with more than 10% missing for discussion with Mike or Joanna before proceeding with analysis.
In R:
data |>
  summarise(across(everything(), ~ sum(is.na(.)))) |>
  pivot_longer(everything(), names_to = "variable", values_to = "n_missing") |>
  mutate(pct_missing = round(n_missing / nrow(data) * 100, 1)) |>
  filter(n_missing > 0) |>
  arrange(desc(n_missing))

title: 12  Data Management
text:
12.5 Data Cleaning Standards
Data cleaning scripts (code/01_data_cleaning.qmd or equivalent) should follow these standards:

Never modify raw data. Read from data/raw/, write cleaned files to data/cleaned/.
Document every decision. When you recode, drop, or transform data, add a comment in the code explaining why.
Check merges carefully. After any merge, verify that the row count and key variables look as expected. Document the check in the script header.
Produce a cleaning log. At the end of the cleaning script, output a summary of what was done: original N, final N, variables added, variables recoded, missing data summary.
Have your cleaning code reviewed. Follow the quality control process described in the Statistical Analysis chapter.

title: 12  Data Management
text:
12.6 A Note on Sensitive Data
Variables that could identify participants — names, addresses, birthdates, school names in small samples, teacher names — require special handling. See the Being a Team Member chapter for data security requirements. As a general rule:

Strip identifying variables from analysis datasets as early as possible in the cleaning process
Keep a separate, secure crosswalk file linking IDs to identifying information if re-identification is ever needed
Store crosswalk files in Yale OneDrive or Storage @ Yale, never in the project GitHub repository

title: 13  Data Wrangling with tidyverse
text:
13.1 Reading Data
The tidyverse is a collection of R packages designed for data science that share a common philosophy and syntax. Rather than learning many unrelated packages, the tidyverse gives you a coherent set of tools that work well together. Load it with:
This loads several packages at once, including dplyr for data manipulation and ggplot2 for visualization.
Use read_csv() to read a CSV file into R:
The here() function constructs file paths relative to the project root, so your code works regardless of where the project is stored on someone’s computer. Always use here() rather than hardcoded paths.
For Excel files, use the readxl package:
For Stata files, use the haven package:

title: 13  Data Wrangling with tidyverse
text:
library(tidyverse)
library(here)

data <- read_csv(here("data", "cleaned", "wave1_cleaned.csv"))


library(readxl)

data <- read_excel(here("data", "raw", "survey_data.xlsx"))

library(haven)

data <- read_dta(here("data", "raw", "survey_data.dta"))

title: 13  Data Wrangling with tidyverse
text:
13.2 Viewing Data
After loading data, get a quick overview:
# Print the first few rows
head(data)

# See dimensions (rows and columns)
dim(data)

# See variable names
names(data)

# See variable types and a preview
glimpse(data)

# Summary statistics for all variables
summary(data)

title: 13  Data Wrangling with tidyverse
13  Data Wrangling with tidyverse
13  Data Wrangling with tidyverse
text:
13.5 Saving Data
Save cleaned datasets to data/cleaned/ using write_csv() for cross-software compatibility, or saveRDS() to preserve R-specific attributes:
# For sharing across software
write_csv(data_cleaned, here("data", "cleaned", "wave1_cleaned.csv"))

# For use within R only (preserves factors, labels, etc.)
saveRDS(data_cleaned, here("data", "cleaned", "wave1_cleaned.rds"))
Use write_csv() when the cleaned data will be read by Stata, SPSS, or Mplus. Use saveRDS() when the data will only be used in R — it is faster and preserves variable types exactly.

title: 14  AI Prompt Examples
text:
Recoding missing values:
I am working in R using tidyverse. My dataset has variables coded 
-99 (skipped), -98 (not applicable), and -97 (data not available). 
I want to recode all three to NA before analysis. The data are in 
a dataframe called `data`. Write code to do this and then produce 
a summary showing how many NAs each variable has.
Merging datasets:
I have two dataframes in R: `student_data` with one row per student 
(N=342, key variable: student_id) and `school_data` with one row 
per school (N=18, key variable: school_id). Each student has a 
school_id variable. I want to merge school-level variables into 
the student dataframe. Write tidyverse code to do this and include 
checks to verify the merge worked correctly — row counts before 
and after, and a check for any unmatched IDs.
Reverse scoring:
I have a scale in R with 10 items (sel_1 through sel_10). Items 
3, 6, and 9 are reverse scored on a 1-5 scale. Write tidyverse 
code to reverse score these items and create new variables named 
with the _r suffix, then compute a mean composite score across 
all 10 items (using the reversed versions where applicable) 
excluding cases with more than 20% missing data.

title: 15  Statistical Analysis
text: 15.1 Statistical Software

title: 15  Statistical Analysis
text:
Excel: Whenever possible, data should be stored in Excel or CSV format, as these are the most accessible formats for sharing across software platforms.
R: R has the most flexibility of all our stats software and is very powerful. It also comes with a steep learning curve. However, AI developments have made R dramatically more accessible, so using R is highly encouraged. See the R Workflows section for lab-specific guidance.
