Mplus: We use Mplus regularly — mostly for analyses including Monte Carlo simulations for assessing statistical power, path analysis, structural equation modeling (SEM), multilevel modeling (MLM), mixture modeling, and approaches that blend these techniques (e.g., multilevel SEM). This is an especially useful package for examining psychometrics. See the Mplus Workflows section for templates and conventions.
Stata: A very flexible and comprehensive stats package. Its power is in its balance of the range of analyses it can conducted and it’s accessibility. On the latter point, it has a GUI interface that allows for point-and-click, and its code language is relatively simple and easy to execute. See the Stata Workflows section for lab-specific guidance.
SPSS: In the past, we used SPSS much more than we do now. Since SPSS does not keep as current with statistical advances as the other software mentioned, we have limited this use. We now mostly use SPSS for data processing and management, data exploration, and basic analyses such as descriptive statistics.

title: 15  Statistical Analysis
text: 15.2 Using AI for Data Analysis

title: 15  Statistical Analysis
section: 15.3 Reminder on Using AI for Data Analysis
text:
15.3 Reminder on Using AI for Data Analysis
Make sure to review the “General Prompting Principles” in the Working with AI chapter. AI tools like Claude can dramatically accelerate data analysis work, especially for writing and debugging code. When used well, AI is like having a highly knowledgeable collaborator available 24/7. A few principles from the Working with AI chapter worth reiterating:

Never share identifiable participant data with AI tools. You can share non-ePHI data in Yale’s Clarity only. Otherwise, paste only anonymized or synthetic examples when asking for help with data-related code. You can create such examples in Clarity if it’s helpful.
Always review and understand the code AI produces before running it. Do not run code you cannot explain. You can always use AI to help you understand the code by asking it to explain it.
AI output should be treated like a first draft: Check it, test it, and own it.
Note AI contributions in your code header as you would note any collaborator’s contribution (e.g., “Analysis code developed with Claude assistance”).


15.3.1 IDEs
An IDE (Integrated Development Environment) is the software application you use to write and run code. The lab’s recommended IDE is Positron, developed by Posit (the makers of RStudio). Positron works well with R, supports Quarto documents, and integrates cleanly with GitHub. It is the successor to RStudio and is what new team members should install. See the Getting Started chapter for installation instructions.
Cursor is an alternative IDE with deeper AI integration that some team members use for more AI-assisted coding workflows.

title: 15  Statistical Analysis
text:
15.4 Quality Control for Analyses
It is inevitable that at some point we will make mistakes in coding and running analyses, especially when there are many steps involved in producing output. Here is our process for managing quality control:

At the beginning of all code, indicate the code author(s) and contact information.
Add a note in the analysis indicating whether and how you checked that the code worked properly. For example, when using merge code, indicate that you checked N and descriptives and that everything lined up.
Have another team member check your code. Suggested reviewers by software:

SPSS: Mike, Joanna
Mplus: Mike
Stata: Mike
R: Mike

When the reviewer checks the code, indicate so in the code header (e.g., “Code reviewed by Michael Strambler”).
After summarizing findings in narrative and/or table format, use AI to check for any misreporting.

title: 15  Statistical Analysis
text:
15.5 Statistical Language
See the Data Analysis Primer for descriptions of common statistics we use. A few things are worth emphasizing here.

15.5.1 Statistical Significance
Statistical significance is a concept that is often misinterpreted — especially by a lay audience, but also by researchers — as an effect or difference being meaningful. A statistical effect and a meaningful or practical effect can be entirely different things, especially when effects are small. It is better to refer to statistical significance for what it really means: a statistical effect or a statistical difference. For example:

The two groups were statistically different.
We interpreted a p-value of <.05 as a statistical effect.



15.5.2 Effect Sizes
The real meaning of an effect is expressed through an effect size and its interpretation. An effect size is a statistic that indicates the strength of an effect. This can be a regression coefficient (b or B), mean difference, Cohen’s d, or any other measure that captures an effect’s magnitude. In terms of judging whether an effect size is “big” or “small,” it is best to place it in the context of other research — compare your effect to effects from similar findings (e.g., “The Cohen’s d of .30 is comparable to the findings of Smith (2019) who found effect sizes ranging between .27 and .35”).

title: 16  Visualization with ggplot2
text:
16.1 Basic Plot Structure
ggplot2 is R’s primary visualization package and one of the most widely used data visualization tools in data science. It builds plots layer by layer using a consistent grammar. Every ggplot starts with ggplot(), specifies which variables map to which visual properties using aes(), and then adds layers using geom_*() functions.
Load it as part of the tidyverse:
Every ggplot has three essential components:
Layers are added with +. Each additional layer refines or adds to the plot.

title: 16  Visualization with ggplot2
text:
Data — the dataframe you are plotting
Aesthetics (aes()) — which variables map to which visual properties (x axis, y axis, color, size, etc.)
Geometry (geom_*()) — what type of plot to draw

ggplot(data, aes(x = grade, y = sel_total_mean)) +
  geom_boxplot()

title: 16  Visualization with ggplot2
text:
16.2 Common Plot Types

16.2.1 Histogram
Use for visualizing the distribution of a single continuous variable:
ggplot(data, aes(x = sel_total_mean)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 20) +
  labs(
    x = "SEL Total Mean",
    y = "Count",
    title = "Distribution of SEL Scores"
  )


16.2.2 Bar Chart
Use for comparing means or counts across groups:
data |>
  group_by(grade) |>
  summarise(mean_sel = mean(sel_total_mean, na.rm = TRUE)) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = factor(grade), y = mean_sel)) +
  geom_col() +
  labs(x = "Grade", y = "Mean SEL Score")


16.2.3 Box Plot
Use for showing distributions across groups:
ggplot(data, aes(x = factor(grade), y = sel_total_mean)) +
  geom_boxplot() +
  labs(x = "Grade", y = "SEL Total Mean")


16.2.4 Scatter Plot
Use for examining the relationship between two continuous variables:
ggplot(data, aes(x = rdg_score, y = sel_total_mean)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.5) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm") +
  labs(x = "Reading Score", y = "SEL Total Mean")
The alpha argument controls point transparency (0 = invisible, 1 = solid), which helps when points overlap. geom_smooth(method = "lm") adds a linear regression line with a confidence interval.


16.2.5 Line Plot
Use for showing change over time or across waves:
data |>
  group_by(wave, grade) |>
  summarise(mean_sel = mean(sel_total_mean, na.rm = TRUE)) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = wave, y = mean_sel, color = factor(grade), group = factor(grade))) +
  geom_line() +
  geom_point() +
  labs(x = "Wave", y = "Mean SEL Score", color = "Grade")

title: 16  Visualization with ggplot2
text:
16.3 Labels and Titles
Always label your axes and add a title. Use labs():
ggplot(data, aes(x = grade, y = sel_total_mean)) +
  geom_boxplot() +
  labs(
    title = "SEL Scores by Grade",
    subtitle = "Wave 1 data, N = 342",
    x = "Grade",
    y = "SEL Total Mean Score",
    caption = "Source: CWER Wave 1 Survey"
  )

title: 16  Visualization with ggplot2
text:
16.4 Themes
Themes control the overall appearance of the plot. theme_minimal() is a clean, publication-friendly option:
ggplot(data, aes(x = grade, y = sel_total_mean)) +
  geom_boxplot() +
  labs(x = "Grade", y = "SEL Total Mean") +
  theme_minimal()
Other useful themes: theme_classic(), theme_bw().

title: 16  Visualization with ggplot2
text:
16.5 Saving Figures
Save figures to output/figures/ using ggsave(). Always assign your plot to an object first:
p <- ggplot(data, aes(x = factor(grade), y = sel_total_mean)) +
  geom_boxplot() +
  labs(x = "Grade", y = "SEL Total Mean") +
  theme_minimal()

ggsave(
  here("output", "figures", "sel_by_grade.png"),
  plot = p,
  width = 6,
  height = 4,
  dpi = 300
)
Use dpi = 300 for publication-quality resolution. Standard figure widths are 6 inches for a single-column figure and 8 inches for a full-width figure.
For PDF output (preferred for manuscripts):
ggsave(
  here("output", "figures", "sel_by_grade.pdf"),
  plot = p,
  width = 6,
  height = 4
)

title: 17  CFA & EFA
text:
17.1 Confirmatory Factor Analysis (CFA) with Mplus
Confirmatory Factor Analysis (CFA) and Exploratory Factor Analysis (EFA) are among the most common analyses in CWER’s psychometric work. CFA tests whether a hypothesized factor structure fits the data. EFA explores the factor structure when the structure is unknown or uncertain. This chapter covers both, with syntax examples and the MplusAutomation workflow for running them from R.
CFA is used when you have a theoretically motivated factor structure and want to test how well it fits your data. For example, you might hypothesize that 12 SEL items measure three latent factors (self-management, social awareness, relationship skills) and use CFA to test whether this structure is supported.

title: 17  CFA & EFA
17  CFA & EFA
17  CFA & EFA
text:
17.3 Running CFA/EFA via MplusAutomation
The full R workflow for running CFA from R:
library(tidyverse)
library(here)
library(MplusAutomation)

# 1. Load cleaned data
data <- readRDS(here("data", "cleaned", "wave1_cleaned.rds"))

# 2. Rename to Mplus-compatible names
data_mplus <- data |>
  rename(
    selsm1 = sel_selfmgmt_1,
    selsm2 = sel_selfmgmt_2,
    selsm3 = sel_selfmgmt_3,
    selsm4 = sel_selfmgmt_4,
    selsa1 = sel_socaware_1,
    selsa2 = sel_socaware_2,
    selsa3 = sel_socaware_3,
    selrs1 = sel_relskill_1,
    selrs2 = sel_relskill_2,
    selrs3 = sel_relskill_3,
    schlid = school_id,
    stuid  = student_id
  )

# 3. Write Mplus data file
prepareMplusData(
  data_mplus,
  filename = here("code", "mplus", "wave1_data.dat")
)

# 4. Run model (assumes cfa_model.inp is already written)
runModels(here("code", "mplus", "cfa_model.inp"))

# 5. Read results
output <- readModels(here("code", "mplus", "cfa_model.out"))

# 6. Extract fit indices
fit <- output$summaries |>
  select(CFI, TLI, RMSEA_Estimate, RMSEA_90CI_LB, RMSEA_90CI_UB, SRMR)

# 7. Extract standardized loadings
loadings <- output$parameters$stdyx.standardized |>
  filter(grepl("BY", paramHeader))

# 8. Save results
write_csv(fit, here("output", "tables", "cfa_fit.csv"))
write_csv(loadings, here("output", "tables", "cfa_loadings.csv"))

title: 17  CFA & EFA
text:
17.4 Model Comparison
To compare nested models (e.g., one-factor vs. three-factor), use the DIFFTEST procedure with WLSMV:
In the baseline model input, add:
SAVEDATA:
  DIFFTEST = baseline.dat;
In the comparison model input, add:
ANALYSIS:
  DIFFTEST = baseline.dat;
With MplusAutomation, run both models and compare fit indices:
# Run both models
runModels(here("code", "mplus"))

# Read all output
all_output <- readModels(here("code", "mplus"), recursive = FALSE)

# Compare fit
fit_comparison <- lapply(all_output, function(m) m$summaries) |>
  bind_rows(.id = "model") |>
  select(model, CFI, TLI, RMSEA_Estimate, SRMR)

print(fit_comparison)

title: 18  Multilevel Modeling
text:
18.1 When to Use Multilevel Modeling
Multilevel modeling is appropriate when your data have a nested structure and observations within the same group are likely more similar to each other than to observations in other groups. Common examples in CWER work include:
Ignoring this nesting and running a standard regression violates the assumption of independent observations, which inflates Type I error rates and produces incorrect standard errors. Multilevel modeling accounts for the non-independence by estimating variance at each level of the hierarchy.

title: 18  Multilevel Modeling
text:
Students nested within classrooms or schools
Repeated measures nested within individuals
Teachers nested within schools

title: 18  Multilevel Modeling
text:
18.2 Using R
lme4 is the standard R package for multilevel (hierarchical) modeling. Use it for data with a nested structure — students within classrooms, classrooms within schools, repeated measures within individuals.
Install and load it:
install.packages("lme4")
library(lme4)

18.2.1 The Intraclass Correlation Coefficient
Before fitting a full multilevel model, run an unconditional means model (also called a null model or intercept-only model) to estimate the intraclass correlation coefficient (ICC). The ICC tells you how much of the total variance in the outcome is attributable to the grouping variable.
model_null <- lmer(sel_total_mean ~ 1 + (1 | school_id), data = data)
summary(model_null)
Calculate the ICC using the performance package:
library(performance)
icc(model_null)
An ICC of .10 means 10% of the variance in SEL scores is between schools. A general rule of thumb is that multilevel modeling is warranted when the ICC is .05 or higher, though you should use judgment based on the research context.


18.2.2 Model Syntax
lme4 uses a formula syntax that extends standard R model syntax. Fixed effects are specified as in standard regression. Random effects are specified in parentheses:
outcome ~ fixed_effects + (random_effects | grouping_variable)
The 1 inside the parentheses represents a random intercept — allowing the mean outcome to vary across groups.


18.2.3 Adding Level-1 Predictors
Level-1 predictors are variables measured at the individual level (e.g., student-level variables):
model_1 <- lmer(sel_total_mean ~ grade + (1 | school_id), data = data)
summary(model_1)


18.2.4 Adding Level-2 Predictors
Level-2 predictors are variables measured at the group level (e.g., school-level variables):
model_2 <- lmer(sel_total_mean ~ grade + school_size + (1 | school_id), data = data)
summary(model_2)


18.2.5 Random Slope Model
A random slope model allows the effect of a predictor to vary across groups:
model_3 <- lmer(sel_total_mean ~ grade + (1 + grade | school_id), data = data)
summary(model_3)
This estimates both a random intercept (mean SEL varies by school) and a random slope (the effect of grade on SEL varies by school).


18.2.6 Extracting Results
Use broom.mixed to extract fixed effect estimates in a tidy format:
library(broom.mixed)

tidy(model_2, effects = "fixed", conf.int = TRUE)
This produces a dataframe with one row per fixed effect, including estimates, standard errors, and confidence intervals — easy to pass to a table-making function.


18.2.7 Comparing Models
Use anova() to compare nested models via likelihood ratio test:
anova(model_1, model_2)
This tests whether adding school-level predictors significantly improves model fit. Note that lme4 uses REML estimation by default for variance components, but switches to ML for model comparisons — anova() handles this automatically.


18.2.8 Checking Assumptions
Examine residual plots to check model assumptions:
# Residuals vs fitted
plot(model_2)

# QQ plot of residuals
qqnorm(resid(model_2))
qqline(resid(model_2))

# Random effects distribution
dotplot(ranef(model_2, condVar = TRUE))

title: 18  Multilevel Modeling
text:
18.3 Using Mplus

18.3.1 When to Use Mplus
lme4 is well-suited for standard multilevel models with continuous outcomes. Consider using Mplus instead when:

You need full information maximum likelihood (FIML) for missing data
Your model involves latent variables or measurement models
You need multilevel SEM
You are estimating models with binary or ordinal outcomes in a multilevel framework
You need Monte Carlo simulation for power analysis

See the Mplus chapter for guidance on those cases.

title: 19  Path Analysis
text:
19.1 What is Path Analysis?
Path analysis is used to test hypothesized relationships among a set of observed variables. Unlike simple regression, path analysis allows you to model multiple outcomes simultaneously, include mediating variables, and test complex causal chains in a single model. CWER uses path analysis to examine how school, classroom, and student-level variables relate to academic and social-emotional outcomes.
A path model specifies directional relationships (paths) among observed variables. Each path is essentially a regression coefficient. The advantage over running separate regressions is that path analysis accounts for the correlations among predictors and outcomes simultaneously, providing a more accurate picture of the relationships in the data.
For example, you might hypothesize that professional development predicts teacher SEL practices, which in turn predicts student SEL outcomes — a mediation model. Path analysis tests this entire chain in one model.

title: 19  Path Analysis
20  Item Response Theory (IRT)
text:
20.1 Key IRT Concepts
Item Response Theory (IRT) models the relationship between a latent trait (e.g., reading ability, SEL competence) and the probability of a particular response to each item. Unlike classical test theory, which focuses on scale-level statistics, IRT models item-level characteristics — how difficult each item is, how well it discriminates between respondents at different trait levels, and whether items function differently across groups. CWER uses IRT primarily for psychometric evaluation of scales and instruments.
Latent trait (θ) — the underlying construct being measured, expressed on a standardized scale (typically mean = 0, SD = 1).
Item difficulty (b) — the point on the latent trait scale where a respondent has a 50% probability of endorsing the item (for binary items) or a particular response (for polytomous items). Higher values indicate harder or less frequently endorsed items.
Item discrimination (a) — how sharply an item distinguishes between respondents above and below the difficulty threshold. Higher discrimination means the item is more sensitive to differences in the latent trait.
Item characteristic curve (ICC) — a plot showing the probability of endorsing an item as a function of the latent trait. A well-discriminating item has a steep ICC.

title: 20  Item Response Theory (IRT)
text:
20.2 IRT Models
The appropriate IRT model depends on your item format:







Item Format
Common Models




Binary (yes/no, correct/incorrect)
1PL (Rasch), 2PL, 3PL


Ordinal (Likert)
Graded Response Model (GRM), Partial Credit Model (PCM)


Nominal (unordered categories)
Nominal Response Model



For most CWER work with Likert-scale items, the Graded Response Model (GRM) is appropriate. For binary items, the 2PL model is the standard choice. The 1PL (Rasch) model constrains all discriminations to be equal — use it when parsimony is a priority and the equal-discrimination assumption can be justified.

title: 20  Item Response Theory (IRT)
21  Power Analysis
text:
21.1 What is Monte Carlo Simulation?
Monte Carlo simulation in Mplus is used to determine the sample size needed to detect effects of a specified magnitude with adequate statistical power. Rather than relying on simple formulas (which assume simple designs), Monte Carlo simulation accounts for the complexity of your actual model — including latent variables, missing data, clustering, and multiple outcomes. CWER uses Monte Carlo simulation for grant proposals and study design decisions.
Monte Carlo simulation works by:
This gives you a direct, empirical estimate of power for your specific model and design.

title: 21  Power Analysis
text:
Specifying the population model — the “true” parameter values you expect based on theory or prior research
Generating a large number of datasets (typically 1000–10000) from that population model at a given sample size
Fitting the model to each dataset and recording whether parameters are statistically significant
Calculating power as the proportion of datasets in which the parameter of interest is significant

title: 11  Project Organization
text:
11.2 Technical Coding Organization

11.2.1 The Core Principle
For technical organization, every project has two types of files, and keeping them separate is the foundation of everything else:

Input files are fixed. Data that comes from outside the project such as partner-provided files, downloaded datasets, or exported survey data, goes in data/raw/ and is never modified by your scripts. If you need a cleaned version, your script reads the original, transforms it, and saves the result to data/cleaned/.
Outputs are reproducible. Everything your scripts produce such as figures, tables, and rendered reports goes in output/ and can always be regenerated by rerunning the script that created it. Because of this, they are not committed to GitHub.

When you know that everything in data/raw/ is an original, untouched input, you can always trust it as the ground truth for the project.


11.2.2 Technical Project Structure
A typical CWER project looks like this:
my-project/
├── data/                   # All data files
│   ├── raw/                # Original inputs — scripts never modify these
│   ├── cleaned/            # Processed data produced by cleaning scripts
│   └── README.md           # Documents where each raw file came from
├── code/                   # Analysis scripts (.qmd, .do, .R, .inp)
│   └── exploratory/        # One-off analyses and sanity checks
├── output/                 # All generated outputs
│   ├── tables/             # Tables (.docx, .xlsx, .csv)
│   └── figures/            # Figures (.png, .pdf)
├── docs/                   # Reports, presentations, manuscripts
└── README.md               # Project overview



The CWER project folder structure as it appears in the Positron Explorer panel.


Not every project needs every subfolder. Adapt as needed, but keep the core separation between data/raw/ and everything else intact.


11.2.3 The data/ Folder

11.2.3.1 data/raw/
The data/raw/ folder holds files that come from outside the project — things your scripts read but never modify. This includes:

Survey data exported from Qualtrics or a partner system
Data received from school districts or community partners
Publicly available datasets downloaded from external sources
Codebooks and metadata provided by partners

Treat everything in data/raw/ as read-only. Scripts read from it but never write to it. If a raw file needs to be corrected, replace it and document the change in data/README.md. Do not silently overwrite it.


11.2.3.2 data/cleaned/
The data/cleaned/ folder holds processed versions of raw data produced by your cleaning scripts. These are intermediate files that other scripts will use as inputs, such as merged datasets, recoded variables, reshaped files, and so on. Because cleaned files are generated by code, they can always be recreated by rerunning the cleaning script.


11.2.3.3 data/README.md
Always include a data/README.md that documents where each raw file came from, when it was received, and any relevant notes. This takes two minutes to write and is invaluable six months later when you are trying to remember which version of a dataset came from which partner, or which Qualtrics export corresponds to which wave of data collection.
Example data/README.md:
# Data Sources

## raw/care4kids_survey_wave1.csv
- Source: CT Office of Early Childhood, received from Jane Smith via Yale OneDrive File Transfer
- Received: 2025-09-15
- Notes: Wave 1 only (fall 2025). N=342. Includes provider ID but no names.

## raw/sel_codebook_v3.xlsx
- Source: Internal; developed by CWER team
- Last updated: 2025-08-01
- Notes: Version 3 adds items from the revised teacher observation protocol



11.2.4 The code/ Folder
Analysis scripts are numbered to reflect the order of the pipeline:
code/
├── 01_data_cleaning.qmd
├── 02_descriptives.qmd
├── 03_mlm_analysis.qmd
├── 04_tables_figures.qmd
└── exploratory/
The two-digit prefix means scripts appear in pipeline order when you list the folder. Script 01 reads from data/raw/ and writes cleaned files to data/cleaned/. Script 02 reads from data/cleaned/ and produces descriptives. A new team member can look at the file list and understand the overall flow without reading any code.
Numbers reflect logical order, not strict dependencies. Script 04 might read outputs from both scripts 01 and 03. The numbering just helps you understand the overall structure.
When you add a new script, assign the next available number. If you retire a script, do not renumber the others. Leave gaps rather than creating confusion about which script a particular output came from.

11.2.4.1 Script Headers
Every script should begin with a header block identifying the author, purpose, inputs, and outputs:
# ============================================================
# Script:  01_data_cleaning.qmd
# Author:  Your Name (your.email@yale.edu)
# Created: 2025-10-01
# Purpose: Clean and merge Wave 1 survey data
# Inputs:  data/raw/care4kids_survey_wave1.csv
# Outputs: data/cleaned/wave1_cleaned.rds
# AI:      Code developed with Claude assistance
# Review:  Reviewed by Michael Strambler, 2025-10-05
# ============================================================
The AI and Review lines reflect our commitments to transparency and quality control described in the Statistical Analysis chapter.


11.2.4.2 Script Status
Add a status field to each script’s YAML header to signal where it is in its lifecycle:
---
title: "Multilevel Model Analysis"
author: "Your Name"
date: today
status: development
---







Status
Meaning




development
In active development; outputs may change


finalized
Outputs are publication-ready; changes should be deliberate


deprecated
Superseded by a newer script; kept for reference



When results are heading toward a report or publication, mark the relevant scripts finalized. This signals to collaborators that any changes should be intentional and that outputs should be re-verified if the script is rerun.


11.2.4.3 Exploratory Scripts
Think of the code/exploratory/ subfolder as a playground for for one-off analyses, sanity checks, and ideas you are testing. Exploratory scripts follow relaxed rules with no number prefixes required, and nothing else in the project depends on their outputs. This makes it safe to experiment without worrying about breaking the main pipeline. If an exploratory analysis turns out to be valuable, promote it to a numbered script in the main directory.



11.2.5 The output/ Folder
All generated outputs go in output/, organized into two subfolders:
output/
├── tables/
│   ├── descriptives_table.docx
│   └── mlm_results_table.docx
└── figures/
    ├── figure1_histogram.png
    └── figure2_scatter.pdf
Keep file names descriptive so their purpose is clear without opening them. Avoid generic names like table1.docx or figure_final.png.


11.2.6 The docs/ Folder
The docs/ folder holds reports, presentations, manuscripts, and other documents intended for external audiences or stakeholders. These are the human-readable products of the project — the things you send to partners, funders, or journals. Unlike output/, which holds raw generated files, docs/ holds polished, finalized deliverables.


11.2.7 Cross-Software Data Format
When data produced by one software needs to be read by another — for example, R producing a dataset that Mplus will read, or Stata producing a file that R will analyze — save in a format both can read:

CSV is the most universally accessible format and the default choice for sharing data across R, Stata, Mplus, and SPSS
Excel (.xlsx) is acceptable when the recipient is more comfortable with spreadsheets, but be aware that formatting and formulas can cause problems when read programmatically
When saving from R for Mplus input, use MplusAutomation package functions to ensure the format is correct

Within a single software environment, use native formats where appropriate (.rds for R objects, .dta for Stata) to preserve data types and attributes.
